original_listing.title_expected: Rhizobium inoculant, nitrogen, and phosphorus improves photosynthesis and water use efficiency in soybean under drought stress
original_listing.authors_expected: Singh, M., Singh, V., Khera, D. et al.

actual_publication_found.title: Inoculant, nitrogen and phosphorus improves photosynthesis and water-use efficiency in soybean production

publications.abstract: Field studies were conducted in 2017 and 2018 seasons in two agroecologies (Angonia and Ruace) in Mozambique to evaluate the effects of Bradyrhizobium diazoefficiens strain USDA110 (formerly known as Bradyrhizobium japonicum) inoculant, nitrogen and phosphorus on nodulation, physiology and yield of non-promiscuous (Safari) and promiscuous (TGx 1740-2F) soybean varieties. Data on transpiration, photosynthesis, leaf area index, radiation interception and WUE from the beginning of flowering to maturity were collected. Photosynthesis rate and water-use efficiency (WUE) were distinct with variety, growth stages and inputs within agroecologies. At one site in 2017, inoculated soybean photosynthesized more at 22.8 μmol/m2/s leading to better WUE of 3.6 that corresponded to 2894 kg/ha yield. Overall, soybean WUE was higher when inoculated than N-treated, while P application yielded better. Nitrogen is a key element in photosynthesis process as it affects leaf chloroplasts size, number and composition, and inoculation promotes BNF process that avails more N for plant uptake resulting in better leaf development.
key_findings: Inoculated soybean had elevated photosynthesis rate (22.8 μmol/m2/s)
WUE of 3.6 corresponded to 2894 kg/ha yield
Soybean WUE was higher when inoculated than N-treated
Inoculation promotes biological nitrogen fixation leading to better leaf development
publications.clarification_note: The ID listed as 'Engoke 2021' in incomplete_publications.json refers to Canon Engoke Norris Savala (C.E.N. Savala), the corresponding author. The publication year is 2021, not 2023. The journal is Journal of Agricultural Science, not Physiologia Plantarum. Singh et al. 2023 publication in Physiologia Plantarum was not found; this appears to be a data entry error or confusion with another publication.

Cambridge Core blog featured this as Editorial Highlight in 2021
Work supported by CGIAR Research Program on Grain Legumes and Dryland Cereals
Related publications by same authors include 2022 IntechOpen chapter on inoculant formulation
No Singh, Khera authors found in this publication - appears to be data entry error in original list
publications.recommendation: This is the correct publication. Update database records to reflect accurate authors (Savala, Wiredu, Okoth, Kyei-Boahen), year (2021), and journal (The Journal of Agricultural Science). Full text available via DOI at Cambridge Core.

publications.title: A stepwise approach to facilitate adoption of climate smart practices for smallholder coffee production in Uganda
author_affiliations: International Institute of Tropical Agriculture, Kampala, Uganda
Department of Agricultural Production, College of Agricultural and Environmental Sciences, Makerere University, Kampala, Uganda
publications.abstract: Adoption of Climate-smart Agricultural Practices (CSA) enhances crop productivity and livelihoods. Smallholder coffee farmers in Uganda receive training on good agricultural practices (GAPs) through extension programs, but adoption remains low. Adopting all 'best practices' at once may be unrealistic for resource-limited, risk-averse smallholders. This study tested a Stepwise approach, breaking GAPs into manageable, incremental investments. Field trials were conducted in Luweero (Central) and Sironko (East) districts of Uganda, with 16 demonstration sites using randomized treatments and the control (farmer practice), divided into four steps. Each site served 25–30 farmers through experiential learning. A co-design method ensured farmer involvement in the design, development, and testing for sustainability. Results from two harvests (2018–2019) showed significant cumulative yield gains over the control. Arabica yields increased by 31%, 43%, 54% and 65% across Steps 1-4. Robusta showed gains of 7%, 22%, 23% and 39% respectively. Marginal rate of returns (MRR) was relatively high for Step 1 (563%), 2 (169%) and 4 (122%) for Robusta coffee, and 221%, 217% and 485% for Step 2, 3 and 4 for Arabica coffee respectively. The Stepwise approach demonstrated improved yield gains and increased farmer income.
publications.geographic_focus: Uganda (Luweero Central and Sironko East districts)
publications.study_type: Action research and case study (field trials with randomized treatments)
publications.methods: The study design is based on theoretical basis for the Stepwise approach, consisting of three main components: (a) co-design and co-development, (b) experiential learning, (c) farmers' feedback, and (d) hypothesis testing. The combination brought about the idea of breaking down the GAPs packages into smaller packages that could be implemented in phases.
key_findings: Arabica coffee yields increased by 31%, 43%, 54% and 65% across Steps 1-4
Robusta coffee showed gains of 7%, 22%, 23% and 39% across Steps 1-4
High marginal rate of returns (MRR) for all steps (122-563%)
Coffee is among the most important cash crops of Uganda with total coffee exports of 345,637MT worth US$ 837 million
16 demonstration sites with 25-30 farmers each through experiential learning
Two harvests (2018-2019) showed significant cumulative yield gains over control
publications.climate_relevance: Central focus on climate-smart agricultural practices for coffee production, addressing climate adaptation and resilience for smallholder farmers

related_publications.title: Step by step to higher yields? Adoption and impacts of a sequenced training approach for climate-smart coffee production in Uganda
related_publications.note: Companion paper evaluating Stepwise approach comprehensively
